fact: Over nine thousand people died, making it the deadliest shipwreck in history
source: Death in the Baltic: The World War II Sinking of the Wilhelm Gustloff, Cathryn J. Prince (2013)

fact: The ship was originally a cruise liner built for 1,500 passengers but carried around ten thousand people
source: The Wilhelm Gustloff was a KdF cruise ship with a normal capacity of 1,463 passengers; estimates of those aboard on Jan 30, 1945 range from 9,000 to over 10,500

fact: Three torpedoes hit the ship just after nine PM on January 30, 1945, fired by a Soviet submarine
source: Soviet submarine S-13, commanded by Alexander Marinesko, fired three torpedoes at approximately 21:16. Widely documented; see also: The Cruelest Night, Christopher Dobson, John Miller, Ronald Payne (1979)

fact: The captain switched on navigation lights, fearing a collision with another vessel, making the ship visible to the submarine
source: Death in the Baltic, Cathryn J. Prince (2013); also documented in Salt to the Sea, Ruta Sepetys (historical note) and multiple naval histories

fact: The ship listed so fast that lifeboats on one side could not be launched, and Baltic water temperature in January was below freezing
source: The ship listed heavily to port within minutes; many lifeboats were frozen to davits or inaccessible. Baltic surface temperatures in late January 1945 were approximately −2°C. See: The Cruelest Night, Dobson, Miller & Payne (1979)

fact: Most of the victims were women and children — German civilians fleeing the Soviet advance across East Prussia
source: Widely documented. The evacuation (Operation Hannibal) moved civilians and wounded soldiers; the Gustloff's passenger manifest was predominantly refugees. See: Operation Hannibal, Wikipedia
